part_subtitle: Morphological Characteristics, Incubation, Hatching, and Applied Importance

items.text: Chapter Six
Morphological Description of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

The egg represents the first stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta, from which the formation of a new generation begins within the field. Although the egg does not feed on the plant and causes no direct damage, it represents a critical stage in the development of infestation because every viable, unparasitized egg can develop into a mining larva that penetrates plant tissues within a short period after hatching.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg represents the first stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta, from which the formation of a new generation begins within the field. Although the egg does not feed on the plant and causes no direct damage, it represents a critical stage in the development of infestation because every viable, unparasitized egg can develop into a mining larva that penetrates plant tissues within a short period after hatching.

items.text: The egg stage is particularly important in integrated pest management because it is the final stage before the larva enters leaves, growing points, stems, or fruits. Once the larva penetrates plant tissue, it becomes more protected from contact insecticides and from many environmental factors and natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg stage is particularly important in [[term:ipm]]integrated pest management[[/term]] because it is the final stage before the larva enters leaves, growing points, stems, or fruits. Once the larva penetrates plant tissue, it becomes more protected from contact insecticides and from many environmental factors and [[term:biological-control]]natural enemies[[/term]].

items.text: The Tuta egg is characterized by the following:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The Tuta egg is characterized by the following:

items: Attached directly to the plant surface.
Its color is close to that of plant tissues.
Its incubation period is affected by temperature.
It may be exposed to predation, parasitism, desiccation, and non-viability.
Its rate of development increases as temperature rises within the suitable range.
It hatches within a few days under warm conditions.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Its color is close to that of plant tissues.
[[prepared]]Its incubation period is affected by temperature.
[[prepared]]It may be exposed to predation, parasitism, desiccation, and non-viability.
[[prepared]]Its rate of development increases as temperature rises within the suitable range.
[[prepared]]It hatches within a few days under warm conditions.

items.text: Therefore, egg monitoring is not merely a description of the insect’s life cycle; it is also an early-warning tool for predicting the emergence of newly hatched larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore, egg monitoring is not merely a description of the insect’s life cycle; it is also an early-warning tool for predicting the emergence of newly hatched larvae.

items.text: Egg Shape
The egg of Tuta absoluta is small, oval or elliptical in shape, and may appear short-cylindrical when viewed from the side.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg of Tuta absoluta is small, oval or elliptical in shape, and may appear short-cylindrical when viewed from the side.

items.text: It is characterized by the following:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is characterized by the following:

items: Its length is greater than its width.
Both ends are rounded rather than pointed.
It adheres directly to plant tissue.
It is not borne on a long stalk.
It is not usually laid in large, organized masses.
Its longitudinal axis is approximately parallel to the surface of the leaf or stem.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Its length is greater than its width.
[[prepared]]Both ends are rounded rather than pointed.
[[prepared]]It is not borne on a long stalk.
[[prepared]]It is not usually laid in large, organized masses.
[[prepared]]Its longitudinal axis is approximately parallel to the surface of the leaf or stem.

items.text: Describing the egg as oval does not conflict with describing it as short-cylindrical, because its apparent shape varies according to the viewing angle. It appears oval when viewed from above, while the body appears elongated and slightly raised when viewed from the side.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Describing the egg as oval does not conflict with describing it as short-cylindrical, because its apparent shape varies according to the viewing angle. It appears oval when viewed from above, while the body appears elongated and slightly raised when viewed from the side.

The usual measurements of a Tuta absoluta egg are approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The usual measurements of a Tuta absoluta egg are approximately:

items.text: The average measurement reported in several studies is approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The average measurement reported in several studies is approximately:

items.text: 0.38 mm long × 0.21 mm wide.
These measurements mean that the egg is far too small to be examined efficiently with the naked eye. Detection requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These measurements mean that the egg is far too small to be examined efficiently with the naked eye. Detection requires:

items: Concentration on young leaves and plant growing points.
A stereomicroscope when confirmation of viability or parasitism is required.

[[prepared]]A stereomicroscope when confirmation of viability or parasitism is required.

items.text: Measurements may vary slightly as a result of differences in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Measurements may vary slightly as a result of differences in:

items: Position of the egg during examination.
Whether the specimen is alive or preserved.

items.text: Egg Color and Its Change During Development
Egg color does not remain constant from oviposition until hatching; rather, it changes gradually as the embryo develops.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg color does not remain constant from oviposition until hatching; rather, it changes gradually as the embryo develops.

items.text: Newly Laid Egg
A newly laid egg is usually:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A newly laid egg is usually:

items.text: This coloration makes the egg difficult to detect on tomato leaves, especially in the presence of plant trichomes, dust, and residues of spray solutions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This coloration makes the egg difficult to detect on tomato leaves, especially in the presence of plant trichomes, dust, and residues of spray solutions.

items.text: Mid-Incubation Period
As embryonic development continues, the color changes to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]As embryonic development continues, the color changes to:

items: Sometimes yellow with a light orange tinge.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Sometimes yellow with a light orange tinge.

items.text: The embryo gradually becomes visible through the semi-transparent eggshell.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The embryo gradually becomes visible through the semi-transparent eggshell.

items.text: Egg Approaching Hatching
Before hatching, the egg becomes darker, and a dark area becomes visible inside it, representing the larval head and head capsule.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before hatching, the egg becomes darker, and a dark area becomes visible inside it, representing the larval head and [[term:head-capsule]]head capsule[[/term]].

items.text: This stage is known as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This stage is known as:

items.text: Blackhead Stage
At this stage, hatching is imminent, especially at high temperatures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At this stage, hatching is imminent, especially at high temperatures.

items.text: The appearance of a small dark area inside the egg does not mean that the egg has been parasitized. In a healthy egg approaching hatching, the larval head appears on one side, whereas parasitized eggs tend to darken more uniformly.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The appearance of a small dark area inside the egg does not mean that the egg has been parasitized. In a healthy egg approaching hatching, the larval head appears on one side, whereas parasitized eggs tend to darken more uniformly.

items.text: Eggshell
The egg is surrounded by an outer shell known as the:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg is surrounded by an outer shell known as the:

items.text: Chorion
This shell performs several functions:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This shell performs several functions:

items: Protecting the embryo from mechanical damage.
Partially isolating the embryo from some external influences.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Partially isolating the embryo from some external influences.

items.text: The presence of the chorion does not mean that the egg is completely protected, because it can still be affected by heat, desiccation, insecticides with ovicidal activity, parasitoids, and predators.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The presence of the chorion does not mean that the egg is completely protected, because it can still be affected by heat, desiccation, insecticides with ovicidal activity, parasitoids, and predators.

items.text: Penetration of control products through the shell varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Penetration of control products through the shell varies according to:

items: Presence of a surfactant or oil.
Position of the egg on the plant.
Presence of trichomes or wax layers on the leaf.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Position of the egg on the plant.
[[prepared]]Presence of trichomes or wax layers on the leaf.

items.text: Oviposition Pattern
The female Tuta absoluta usually lays eggs singly, although two or several eggs may occasionally be found close together in a small, irregular group.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female Tuta absoluta usually lays eggs singly, although two or several eggs may occasionally be found close together in a small, irregular group.

items.text: The female does not usually form large egg masses as occurs in some other insects.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female does not usually form large egg masses as occurs in some other insects.

items.text: Laying eggs singly provides the insect with several advantages:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Laying eggs singly provides the insect with several advantages:

items: Distributing offspring over several parts of the plant.
Reducing competition among newly hatched larvae.
Reducing the probability of losing all eggs at once.
Making eggs more difficult to detect and remove.
Increasing the probability that larvae will reach suitable mining sites.
Reducing exposure of all eggs to a single predator or parasitoid.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Distributing offspring over several parts of the plant.
[[prepared]]Reducing the probability of losing all eggs at once.
[[prepared]]Making eggs more difficult to detect and remove.
[[prepared]]Increasing the probability that larvae will reach suitable mining sites.
[[prepared]]Reducing exposure of all eggs to a single predator or parasitoid.

items.text: The female may lay eggs on several plants within the field, helping to spread the infestation rather than concentrating it on a single plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female may lay eggs on several plants within the field, helping to spread the infestation rather than concentrating it on a single plant.

items.text: Distribution of Eggs on Tomato Plant Parts
The female does not distribute eggs equally among all plant parts, but prefers young leaves and tissues near the plant apex.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female does not distribute eggs equally among all plant parts, but prefers young leaves and tissues near the plant apex.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The commonly reported approximate distribution of eggs is as follows:

items.text: These percentages represent a common pattern reported in the literature and are not fixed values for every field, cultivar, or crop-growth stage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These percentages represent a common pattern reported in the literature and are not fixed values for every field, cultivar, or crop-growth stage.

items.text: Egg distribution is affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg distribution is affected by:

items: Location at which moths enter the field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Location at which moths enter the field.

items.text: Distribution on Leaves
Young leaves represent the principal oviposition sites, especially recently fully expanded leaves located in the upper part of the plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Young leaves represent the principal oviposition sites, especially recently fully expanded leaves located in the upper part of the plant.

items.text: Females prefer these leaves because they:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Females prefer these leaves because they:

items: Are close to the growing point.
Provide tender tissues for newly hatched larvae.
Permit easier penetration of leaf tissue.
Are close to growing points, flowers, and young fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Provide tender tissues for newly hatched larvae.
[[prepared]]Are close to growing points, flowers, and young fruits.

items.text: Before flowering, oviposition occurs more frequently on the lower leaf surface, whereas females may use both upper and lower surfaces to a greater extent after the plant develops and enters flowering and fruiting.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before flowering, oviposition occurs more frequently on the lower leaf surface, whereas females may use both upper and lower surfaces to a greater extent after the plant develops and enters flowering and fruiting.

items.text: Vertical Distribution on the Plant
Most eggs are concentrated in the upper or younger layer of the canopy at the time of oviposition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Most eggs are concentrated in the upper or younger layer of the canopy at the time of oviposition.

items.text: However, the position of the leaf changes as the plant grows. An egg may be laid near the apex, but after several days the leaf may become positioned lower because the stem continues elongating and new leaves are produced above it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the position of the leaf changes as the plant grows. An egg may be laid near the apex, but after several days the leaf may become positioned lower because the stem continues elongating and new leaves are produced above it.

items.text: Inspection should therefore not be limited to the uppermost two leaves, but should include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Inspection should therefore not be limited to the uppermost two leaves, but should include:

items: Both upper and lower surfaces of leaflets.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Both upper and lower surfaces of leaflets.

items.text: Distribution Within the Field
Eggs are not distributed completely at random within the field, but tend to have an aggregated distribution.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs are not distributed completely at random within the field, but tend to have an aggregated distribution.

items.text: At the beginning of invasion, their density increases in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At the beginning of invasion, their density increases in:

items: The side facing an old infested field.
Areas near infested nurseries or greenhouses.
The direction from which moth-carrying winds enter.
The first plants in planting rows.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The side facing an old infested field.
[[prepared]]The direction from which moth-carrying winds enter.

items.text: Open-field studies have shown that egg aggregations are often greater at field edges and that spread along planting rows may occur faster than movement across rows.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Open-field studies have shown that egg aggregations are often greater at field edges and that spread along planting rows may occur faster than movement across rows.

items.text: Egg Incubation Period
The egg incubation period is the time from oviposition on the plant until larval emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg incubation period is the time from oviposition on the plant until larval emergence.

items.text: The egg does not have a fixed incubation period because the rate of embryonic development depends primarily on temperature.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg does not have a fixed incubation period because the rate of embryonic development depends primarily on temperature.

items.text: Under moderate and warm conditions, eggs usually hatch within:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under moderate and warm conditions, eggs usually hatch within:

items.text: 4–6 days.
However, this period may be shortened to approximately two or three days under suitably high temperatures, or extended to more than ten days under low temperatures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, this period may be shortened to approximately two or three days under suitably high temperatures, or extended to more than ten days under low temperatures.

items.text: Effect of Temperature on Incubation Period
Embryonic growth becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range. Therefore, temperature and incubation period are inversely related:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Embryonic growth becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range. Therefore, temperature and incubation period are inversely related:

items.text: > The higher the suitable temperature, the shorter the incubation period; the lower the temperature, the longer the period.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]> The higher the suitable temperature, the shorter the incubation period; the lower the temperature, the longer the period.

rows: No hatching occurred in this study

items.text: The data show that increasing temperature from 15 to 25°C reduced the incubation period from approximately 12 days to approximately 4.5 days.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The data show that increasing temperature from 15 to 25°C reduced the incubation period from approximately 12 days to approximately 4.5 days.

items.text: Differences between the two studies demonstrate that the response of eggs to extreme heat is not identical among all populations and experimental conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Differences between the two studies demonstrate that the response of eggs to extreme heat is not identical among all populations and experimental conditions.

items.text: Results differ because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Results differ because of:

items: Adaptation of insects to local climate.
Differences in host plant or cultivar.
Differences in egg age at the time of temperature exposure.
Constant laboratory temperature versus fluctuating field temperature.
Differences in the definition of egg viability, survival, or hatching percentage.

In an experiment conducted at a relative humidity of 60±10%, the following hatching percentages were recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In an experiment conducted at a relative humidity of 60±10%, the following hatching percentages were recorded:

items.text: However, egg-hatching percentage alone is not sufficient to determine the optimum temperature for the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, egg-hatching percentage alone is not sufficient to determine the optimum temperature for the pest.

items.text: Although high hatching was recorded at 30°C in this experiment, survival from egg to adult declined to approximately 33.33%, compared with approximately 76.67% at 25°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Although high hatching was recorded at 30°C in this experiment, survival from egg to adult declined to approximately 33.33%, compared with approximately 76.67% at 25°C.

items.text: At 35°C, 70% of eggs hatched, but the percentage of individuals completing development from egg to adult did not exceed 10%.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At 35°C, 70% of eggs hatched, but the percentage of individuals completing development from egg to adult did not exceed 10%.

items.text: This demonstrates that rapid hatching or a high hatching percentage in a particular experiment does not mean that the same temperature is optimal for completion of the entire generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This demonstrates that rapid hatching or a high hatching percentage in a particular experiment does not mean that the same temperature is optimal for completion of the entire generation.

Another thermal study recorded egg survival or hatching percentages as follows:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Another thermal study recorded egg survival or hatching percentages as follows:

items.text: No eggs hatched at 35°C in this study.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No eggs hatched at 35°C in this study.

items.text: This differs clearly from the study that recorded 100% hatching at 30°C and 70% at 35°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This differs clearly from the study that recorded 100% hatching at 30°C and 70% at 35°C.

items.text: This scientific difference is important because it shows that temperatures close to the upper threshold produce highly variable results, and a single percentage cannot be considered a fixed rule for all pest populations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This scientific difference is important because it shows that temperatures close to the upper threshold produce highly variable results, and a single percentage cannot be considered a fixed rule for all pest populations.

items.text: The most consistent conclusion across studies is that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The most consistent conclusion across studies is that:

items: Severe cold slows development and reduces hatching.
A temperature of 25°C repeatedly provides a short incubation period and high hatching.
Temperatures of 30–35°C accelerate development, but survival may become unstable.
Prolonged high temperature adversely affects the subsequent stages even when some eggs hatch successfully.

High survival of larvae and pupae after hatching.
A high percentage of generation completion.

items.text: The range of 20–25°C is also among the most suitable ranges for the overall development, survival, and reproduction of the pest, while the optimum estimated by some models lies between 21 and 23°C.

items.text: Effect of Relative Humidity on Eggs
Relative humidity affects water balance within the egg and the embryo’s ability to complete development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Relative humidity affects water balance within the egg and the embryo’s ability to complete development.

items.text: A severe decrease in humidity, particularly when combined with high temperature, increases:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A severe decrease in humidity, particularly when combined with high temperature, increases:

items.text: In a study comparing two thermal and humidity regimes, egg survival reached:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In a study comparing two thermal and humidity regimes, egg survival reached:

items.text: This comparison represents the combined effect of temperature and humidity and does not separate the effect of humidity alone because temperature increased at the same time that humidity declined.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This comparison represents the combined effect of temperature and humidity and does not separate the effect of humidity alone because temperature increased at the same time that humidity declined.

items.text: Moderate humidity, which in many rearing experiments lies around 55–70%, provides favorable conditions for egg development, provided that temperature remains within the suitable range.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moderate humidity, which in many rearing experiments lies around 55–70%, provides favorable conditions for egg development, provided that temperature remains within the suitable range.

items.text: Very high humidity accompanied by continuous wetness or water condensation may lead to:

Reduced gas exchange when the egg is covered with water.
Changes in the efficacy of some control products.

items.text: Effect of Extreme Heat
Eggs may tolerate short periods of heat better than continuous exposure in some cases, but severe heat stress reduces hatching.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs may tolerate short periods of heat better than continuous exposure in some cases, but severe heat stress reduces hatching.

items.text: Heat-stress experiments showed that exposing eggs to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Heat-stress experiments showed that exposing eggs to:

items.text: 44°C for six hours
Significantly reduced hatching percentage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Significantly reduced hatching percentage.

items.text: The degree of damage varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The degree of damage varies according to:

items: Whether the egg is exposed to direct sunlight or shade.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Whether the egg is exposed to direct sunlight or shade.

items.text: Leaf or fruit surface temperature may exceed measured air temperature, especially under direct solar radiation, thereby increasing egg exposure to desiccation and heat stress.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Leaf or fruit surface temperature may exceed measured air temperature, especially under direct solar radiation, thereby increasing egg exposure to desiccation and heat stress.

items.text: Egg Sensitivity to Different Factors
Sensitivity to Cold
Cold conditions lead to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cold conditions lead to:

items.text: At 10°C, the incubation period may extend beyond 20 days, whereas hatching may stop at temperatures close to 7°C in some experiments.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At 10°C, the incubation period may extend beyond 20 days, whereas hatching may stop at temperatures close to 7°C in some experiments.

items.text: Delayed hatching under cold conditions does not necessarily result in the death of all eggs. Some may resume development when temperature rises, depending on the duration and intensity of exposure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Delayed hatching under cold conditions does not necessarily result in the death of all eggs. Some may resume development when temperature rises, depending on the duration and intensity of exposure.

items.text: Sensitivity to High Temperature
High temperatures within the acceptable range shorten the incubation period, but prolonged exposure near the upper threshold causes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High temperatures within the acceptable range shorten the incubation period, but prolonged exposure near the upper threshold causes:

items.text: Conflicting results at 30–35°C demonstrate that the capacity of eggs to hatch does not guarantee normal completion of subsequent development.

items.text: Sensitivity to Desiccation
Eggs exposed on the plant surface are subject to water loss, particularly when the following occur together:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs exposed on the plant surface are subject to water loss, particularly when the following occur together:

items.text: The outer shell reduces water loss but does not prevent it completely.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The outer shell reduces water loss but does not prevent it completely.

items.text: Newly laid eggs or eggs damaged mechanically are more susceptible to desiccation than intact eggs with a complete shell.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Newly laid eggs or eggs damaged mechanically are more susceptible to desiccation than intact eggs with a complete shell.

items.text: Sensitivity to Rainfall and Overhead Irrigation
Heavy rain or overhead-irrigation droplets may mechanically affect some exposed eggs through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Heavy rain or overhead-irrigation droplets may mechanically affect some exposed eggs through:

items: Dislodging them from the plant surface.
Altering the temperature and humidity surrounding them.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Altering the temperature and humidity surrounding them.

items.text: However, adhesion of the egg to plant tissue makes water washing an unreliable control method, and rainfall or overhead irrigation does not remove all eggs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, adhesion of the egg to plant tissue makes water washing an unreliable control method, and rainfall or overhead irrigation does not remove all eggs.

The shell is thin, and eggs may therefore be damaged by:

items.text: For this reason, the egg should be collected together with a small section of the leaf rather than being removed directly.

items.text: Sensitivity to Insecticides
Eggs occur on the plant surface and can therefore be directly exposed to spray solution, but they do not respond equally to all insecticides.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs occur on the plant surface and can therefore be directly exposed to spray solution, but they do not respond equally to all insecticides.

items.text: Sensitivity depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sensitivity depends on:

items: Presence of ovicidal activity in the active ingredient.
Ability of the material to penetrate the chorion.

items.text: Many insecticides used against Tuta are more effective against larvae than eggs. Therefore, spraying an insecticide that is effective against larvae does not necessarily eliminate all eggs present on the plant.

Under the experimental conditions. This result demonstrates partial ovicidal activity but does not indicate complete egg control.

items.text: Sensitivity to Predators
Eggs are suitable prey for several predators present in tomato production systems, particularly certain predatory bugs, including:

items.text: Sensitivity to Egg Parasitoids
Eggs are direct targets of parasitoids belonging to the genus:

items.text: Trichogramma
Species used or studied against Tuta absoluta include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Species used or studied against Tuta absoluta include:

items.text: The female parasitoid lays its egg inside the Tuta egg, after which the parasitoid larva feeds on the embryo and egg contents, preventing the emergence of a Tuta larva.

items.text: Parasitized eggs gradually become dark or black.

items.text: Newly laid eggs are usually more suitable for parasitism than eggs approaching hatching.

items.text: Natural Non-Viability
Not all eggs laid by females hatch. Some may die naturally as a result of:

items.text: This result demonstrates that the egg stage is important for natural population regulation before larval damage occurs.

items.text: Importance of the Egg Stage in Integrated Pest Management
The egg stage represents one of the most important targets in the integrated management of Tuta absoluta for the following reasons:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg stage represents one of the most important targets in the integrated management of Tuta absoluta for the following reasons:

items.text: The Egg Precedes Damage
The egg does not produce mines or holes. Therefore, killing or parasitizing it prevents damage before it begins.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg does not produce [[term:mines]]mines[[/term]] or holes. Therefore, killing or parasitizing it prevents damage before it begins.

items.text: Every egg prevented from hatching means:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Every egg prevented from hatching means:

items: A lower probability of fruit infestation.
Less pressure on the insecticide program.
A reduced population in the next generation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A reduced population in the next generation.

items.text: The Egg Is Located Outside Plant Tissue
The egg is attached to the surface of the leaf, stem, or sepals, whereas the larva becomes concealed inside tissues after hatching.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg is attached to the surface of the leaf, stem, or sepals, whereas the larva becomes concealed inside tissues after hatching.

items.text: Eggs are therefore more exposed to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs are therefore more exposed to:

items.text: The Egg Determines the Timing of Larval Emergence
Examination of egg color and age allows estimation of hatching time.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Examination of egg color and age allows estimation of hatching time.

items.text: New creamy eggs require more time, whereas the appearance of the black head means that larval emergence is imminent.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]New creamy eggs require more time, whereas the appearance of the black head means that larval emergence is imminent.

items.text: This information becomes important when timing:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This information becomes important when timing:

items: Use of products with ovicidal activity.
Use of biological insecticides targeting newly hatched larvae.
Prediction of the appearance of new mines.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Use of biological insecticides targeting newly hatched larvae.
[[prepared]]Prediction of the appearance of new mines.

items.text: The Control Window Becomes Shorter Under Warm Conditions
At 20°C, the egg stage may persist for approximately 7–8 days, whereas eggs may hatch within 2.5–4 days at 30°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At 20°C, the egg stage may persist for approximately 7–8 days, whereas eggs may hatch within 2.5–4 days at 30°C.

items.text: Therefore, the period available for intervention becomes shorter as temperature rises.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore, the period available for intervention becomes shorter as temperature rises.

items.text: Under warm conditions, weekly inspection alone is not sufficient when trap catches are high and eggs are present, because a large proportion of eggs may hatch and larvae may enter leaves between two widely spaced inspections.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under warm conditions, weekly inspection alone is not sufficient when trap catches are high and eggs are present, because a large proportion of eggs may hatch and larvae may enter leaves between two widely spaced inspections.

items.text: Supporting Biological Control
The egg stage is the principal target of Trichogramma parasitoids and egg predators.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg stage is the principal target of Trichogramma parasitoids and egg predators.

items.text: The effectiveness of a Trichogramma program depends on the continued presence of the parasitoid in the field at the same time as newly laid Tuta eggs are present.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The effectiveness of a Trichogramma program depends on the continued presence of the parasitoid in the field at the same time as newly laid Tuta eggs are present.

items.text: Releases made after most eggs have hatched do not control larvae that have already entered plant tissues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Releases made after most eggs have hatched do not control larvae that have already entered plant tissues.

items.text: Relationship Between the Egg and Larval Emergence
After embryonic development is completed, the larva chews through part of the eggshell and emerges onto the plant surface.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]After embryonic development is completed, the larva chews through part of the eggshell and emerges onto the plant surface.

items.text: The newly hatched larva remains exposed for a short period before entering the tissue, and this period may be less than:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The newly hatched larva remains exposed for a short period before entering the tissue, and this period may be less than:

items.text: 90 minutes under some conditions.
During this period, the larva is more exposed to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]During this period, the larva is more exposed to:

items.text: Once it enters the leaf, stem, or fruit, the effectiveness of many external control methods declines.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Once it enters the leaf, stem, or fruit, the effectiveness of many external control methods declines.

items.text: Successful control is therefore linked to two successive stages:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Successful control is therefore linked to two successive stages:

items: 2. Targeting the larva immediately after hatching and before it enters plant tissues.

rows: A dark head visible at one end
Empty transparent or white shell with an emergence opening
Dry, malformed, or brown without normal development
Dark or black in a relatively uniform manner
Collapsed, empty, or bearing a fine puncture
Does not hatch and may change color or its contents may dry

items.text: Empty eggshells are not counted as live eggs when estimating infestation density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Empty eggshells are not counted as live eggs when estimating infestation density.

items.text: Field Egg-Inspection Program
Accurate field inspection includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Accurate field inspection includes:

items: 2. Inspecting both field edges and the interior.
3. Focusing on the side adjacent to old fields.
4. Selecting plants distributed among several rows.
5. Inspecting growing points and young leaves.
6. Turning leaflets over to inspect the lower surface.
8. Inspecting sepals and calyces when fruits are present.
10. Classifying eggs as live, approaching hatching, parasitized, or empty.
11. Recording the number of plants carrying eggs.
12. Linking the results to moth numbers in traps.
13. Repeating inspection at shorter intervals when temperature rises.

items.text: The number of plants carrying eggs may sometimes describe the spread of infestation more clearly than the total number of eggs on a single heavily infested plant.

items.text: Collecting Eggs for Laboratory Examination
The egg should be collected together with a small piece of leaf by following these steps:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg should be collected together with a small piece of leaf by following these steps:

items: 1. Locate the egg using a hand lens.
2. Cut a section of the leaflet around it.
3. Place the specimen in a well-ventilated container.
4. Prevent water condensation inside the container.
5. Record the location, date, and cultivar.
6. Record the egg’s position on the plant.
8. Record emergence of a Tuta larva or egg parasitoid.
9. Do not remove the egg directly with forceps.
10. Do not expose it to sunlight or heat inside a vehicle.

items.text: Emergence of the parasitoid or larva is more accurate than color alone for determining parasitism and viability.

rows: Darker, with appearance of the black head
Usually singly or in small groups
Preventing larval emergence before tissue penetration

items.text: Summary of the Egg Stage

The egg of Tuta absoluta is extremely small and oval or elliptical in shape, measuring approximately 0.35–0.38 mm in length and 0.20–0.22 mm in width. When laid, it is creamy white or pale yellow, then becomes progressively darker as the embryo develops until the black head appears before hatching.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg of Tuta absoluta is extremely small and oval or elliptical in shape, measuring approximately 0.35–0.38 mm in length and 0.20–0.22 mm in width. When laid, it is creamy white or pale yellow, then becomes progressively darker as the embryo develops until the black head appears before hatching.

items.text: The female lays approximately 73% of eggs on leaves, 21% on veins, petioles, and stems, 5% on sepals, and 1% on green fruits. Eggs are commonly concentrated on young leaves and in the upper part of the plant, with greater aggregations at field edges during the early stages of infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female lays approximately 73% of eggs on leaves, 21% on veins, petioles, and stems, 5% on sepals, and 1% on green fruits. Eggs are commonly concentrated on young leaves and in the upper part of the plant, with greater aggregations at field edges during the early stages of infestation.

items.text: The incubation period usually ranges from 4 to 6 days under suitable conditions, but it is strongly affected by temperature. It may extend to approximately 12 days at 15°C, decline to approximately 4.5 days at 25°C, and to 2.5–4 days at 30°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The incubation period usually ranges from 4 to 6 days under suitable conditions, but it is strongly affected by temperature. It may extend to approximately 12 days at 15°C, decline to approximately 4.5 days at 25°C, and to 2.5–4 days at 30°C.

items.text: The most suitable and stable thermal range lies between 20 and 25°C, and 25°C is considered one of the best temperatures for egg development because it combines rapid hatching, high viability, and the ability of subsequent stages to complete development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The most suitable and stable thermal range lies between 20 and 25°C, and 25°C is considered one of the best temperatures for egg development because it combines rapid hatching, high viability, and the ability of subsequent stages to complete development.

items.text: Results at 30–35°C vary among studies. Rapid hatching may occur, but overall survival of the generation declines, especially when high temperature persists or is accompanied by low humidity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Results at 30–35°C vary among studies. Rapid hatching may occur, but overall survival of the generation declines, especially when high temperature persists or is accompanied by low humidity.

items.text: Eggs are also affected by desiccation, extreme heat, cold, insecticides with ovicidal activity, predators, Trichogramma parasitoids, and natural non-viability.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs are also affected by desiccation, extreme heat, cold, insecticides with ovicidal activity, predators, Trichogramma parasitoids, and natural non-viability.

items.text: The egg stage is a principal target in integrated pest management because it occurs outside plant tissues and precedes the onset of damage. Killing or parasitizing the egg prevents larval emergence and penetration into leaves or fruits, thereby reducing damage, larval density, and the next pest generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg stage is a principal target in integrated pest management because it occurs outside plant tissues and precedes the onset of damage. Killing or parasitizing the egg prevents larval emergence and penetration into leaves or fruits, thereby reducing damage, larval density, and the next pest generation.
